meta.author: claude-desk
meta.disclaimer: Coverage memos are second-opinion research notes on @aleabitoreddit's ideas. Personal research tool — not investment advice.

memos.title: The laser everyone else's thesis depends on
memos.dek: Sivers sells the continuous-wave lasers that JBL, GlobalFoundries, Ayar and POET all need — the analyst's favorite name, and his most concentrated bet.

sections.heading: Snapshot
sections.body:
$SIVE makes indium-phosphide continuous-wave (CW) DFB lasers — the light source that co-packaged optics and next-gen pluggable transceivers cannot work without. It is fab-lite, Sweden-listed, and mapped as the laser supplier into $JBL's 1.6T modules, $GFS's reference design, Ayar Labs' NVLink-attached CPO, and [[photonics]] scale-out plays like $POET.

This is the analyst's highest-conviction name — "probably my favorite stock right now" — and also his largest holding, which is exactly why this memo exists: the desk's job is to separate the supply-chain logic (strong) from the book-talking (structural).

Late-week development: three insider purchases landed right after the drop — most notably the CEO buying 1,000,000 SEK of stock on the open market — while Nvidia denied the delay reports that caused the selloff and a sell-side channel check concluded Chinese rivals are 'quite far' from high-power CW lasers.

sections.heading: Thesis
sections.body:
The chokepoint argument is simple: everyone building CPO or 1.6T optics needs CW lasers, few suppliers exist, and Sivers sits on qualification tracks at several of them at once. The most dateable claim is the $JBL leg — per a JPM fireside chat the analyst relayed, JBL's 1.6T LRO qualification should complete between **Aug 2026 and Mar 2027**, followed by mass production with Sivers lasers in the pluggables. AEVA targets high-volume manufacturing around Q4 2026 and $POET H2 2026, so the demand waves are staggered rather than simultaneous.

On top sit the corporate catalysts he cites: an oversubscribed funding round for the fabless ramp, a NASDAQ listing "in the next few quarters," then M&A for TAM expansion. His valuation hook is relative — it trades below $POET while supplying half the ecosystem POET belongs to.

sections.heading: Why own it
sections.body: If the CPO transition happens at all, the discrete CW laser is one of the few places where a small company owns a genuine bottleneck rather than renting a story. The multi-customer structure matters: this is not a one-design-win bet, it is four or five partially independent qualification tracks, any one of which validates the ramp. The NASDAQ listing is a separate, non-fundamental catalyst — a liquidity re-rate that does not require the technology thesis to resolve first.

sections.heading: Risks
sections.body:
Every flag the desk tracks is live here. It is the analyst's largest position, so every reiteration is also an advertisement. The auditor has raised a going-concern flag around the SEK 700M raise, and Ningi Research has published a short thesis — if the cash strain is real, dilution does the damage before any design win pays. Integrated-laser architectures (OpenLight-style, needing no external light source) are the technology kill-shot: they remove the discrete CW supplier from the diagram entirely. And the newest angle — Hesai lidar scrutiny pushing western lidar toward $LITE/$SIVE lasers — is second-order speculation, not orders.

Qualification risk is the dateable one: $JBL slipping past Q1 2027, or a design-out at JBL/GFS/Ayar, breaks the strongest leg of the thesis.

sections.heading: Bottom line
sections.body: Accumulate, on the staggered plan — and this week supplied the tranche trigger the plan was waiting for: a CEO open-market buy into a headline-driven drop, both delay reports denied, and Rosenblatt reporting the short sellers pressuring the sector intend to cover in late July/August. The desk treats that combination as the add-on-dip window. What breaks the thesis is unchanged: insider selling resuming, the NASDAQ listing slipping, or the [[jbl]]/GlobalFoundries partnerships failing to convert to volume orders.

memos.title: The incumbent way to own the laser bottleneck
memos.dek: Lumentum is the lower-torque, lower-drama expression of the same photonics chokepoint — the anchor holding of the sleeve while SIVE and AXTI carry the risk.

sections.heading: Snapshot
sections.body: $LITE builds industrial lasers and the optical components inside datacenter transceivers and telecom gear — the incumbent supplier at the [[photonics]] layer where $SIVE is the challenger. In this coverage universe it plays a specific role: the established, revenue-generating way to own the laser bottleneck without underwriting a micro-cap's balance sheet.

sections.heading: Thesis
sections.body:
The analyst's frame is that laser supply stays bottlenecked **into 2029** — demand from 1.6T pluggables and CPO scale-out compounds faster than laser capacity can be added. His standing heuristic: when the CEO says everything they make gets bought, sell-side bear notes about valuation do not touch the fundamentals. The recent washout gave that frame a live test — LITE fell 4–10% alongside the whole complex on what he reads as cascading margin liquidations plus the SemiAnalysis CPO-delay pieces, none of it name-specific.

A second-order kicker appeared with the Hesai story: US national-security scrutiny of the Chinese lidar maker is bullish for western lidar players, and upstream, for their laser suppliers — LITE and SIVE both.

The week's channel check hardened the supply side of the argument: Chinese laser makers reportedly have no working 200G EMLs and remain far from the high-power CW lasers that CPO needs — meaning the capacity the bears assumed was coming isn't visible yet.

sections.heading: Why own it
sections.body: Same chokepoint as the small-caps, a fraction of the fragility. LITE has scale, customer breadth, and actual current earnings against the same structural shortage. In sleeve terms it is the low-beta anchor: if the photonics thesis is right, LITE re-rates with it; if the thesis is early, LITE's drawdowns are survivable in a way SIVE's may not be.

sections.heading: Risks
sections.body: The bear case that matters is the one SemiAnalysis argued: CPO delays plus hyperscaler order pushouts would hit the growth narrative even if today's business holds. The subtler risk is competitive — LITE currently buys from some of the CW-laser entrants; if names like SIVE graduate from suppliers to rivals on next-gen sockets, the incumbent loses share exactly where the growth was supposed to come from. Note also the evidence quality: this week's citations are washout commentary and a second-order lidar story, not new LITE-specific datapoints.

sections.heading: Bottom line
sections.body: Accumulate — keep building the anchor position on red days and let [[sive]]/[[axti]] carry the torque. Rosenblatt's read that short sellers plan to cover in late July/August suggests the red days are numbered; nothing in the current evidence changes sizing. What would: an actual (not reported-then-denied) CPO slip, or Chinese high-power CW capacity showing up in channel checks.

memos.title: The center of the map is not the trade
memos.dek: NVIDIA is the demand hub every thesis here feeds into — which is precisely why this desk expresses the view through the suppliers, not the hub.

sections.heading: Snapshot
sections.body: $NVDA is the gravitational center of this entire coverage universe: every name in the book either supplies into its chips, manufactures them, connects them, or builds on top of them. It carries a desk verdict not because it is a pick, but because it is the reference point every other verdict is measured against — see [[accelerators]].

sections.heading: Thesis
sections.body: The analyst rarely publishes NVDA-specific work, and that is the point. His edge — and this tool's — lives in the layers beneath: the optics, lasers, memory and substrates where a design win moves a small company's entire valuation rather than a mega-cap's next quarter. This week NVDA featured only as collateral: the selloff he attributes to a misleading Bloomberg piece on Meta compute plus SemiAnalysis bear posts on NVDA supply chains, and the Hesai lidar scrutiny in which NVDA is the partner under review.

sections.heading: Why own it
sections.body: Not the question this desk answers. The honest framing: if you want NVDA exposure, that is a core-portfolio allocation decision made outside this workflow. What this coverage universe offers instead is the derivative trade — names whose fortunes are levered to NVDA's roadmap at multiples of the payoff and multiples of the risk.

sections.heading: Risks
sections.body: For this book, NVDA is the risk factor rather than the position: a genuine NVDA demand shock (hyperscaler capex cuts, an export-control expansion, a credible custom-silicon displacement story) hits every supplier here harder than it hits NVDA itself. The narrative war matters too — this week showed that two bearish supply-chain posts can mark the whole complex down 40–60% from the highs, fundamentals untouched.

sections.heading: Bottom line
sections.body: Watch. Express the view through the suppliers this map surfaces; hold NVDA as a separate decision. The desk would re-rate this into an active call only if the analyst starts publishing NVDA-specific supply or pricing work — that is the falsifier for keeping it parked.

memos.title: The washout he asked for arrived — and he bought it
memos.dek: Applied Optoelectronics ran from the analyst's entry to a multi-bagger, crashed 40–60% with the optical complex, and his own framework said that dip was the entry.

sections.heading: Snapshot
sections.body: $AAOI makes optical transceivers — the modules that convert electrical signals to light so GPU racks can move data at bandwidths copper physically cannot carry. It sits in the [[photonics]] layer as the volume-manufacturing pure play, and it is one of the analyst's two most concentrated holdings alongside $SIVE.

sections.heading: Thesis
sections.body:
The headline claim is aggressive: a ~$1.4B/quarter revenue run-rate by H1 2027 — 800%+ year-over-year growth — driven by hyperscaler transceiver demand, stated off a market cap that makes the multiple look cheap *if* the ramp is real. The analyst himself called the stock overextended after its huge run and predicted range-bound chop until earnings.

Then the washout came: the SemiAnalysis CPO-delay pieces plus the Bloomberg Meta-compute story knocked optical names down 40–60%. His response was to buy heavily — and notably, the desk's own prior verdict had named exactly this condition ("a washout back toward the pre-run range") as the entry trigger. The condition fired; the stance upgraded from watch to accumulate.

sections.heading: Why own it
sections.body: Of the transceiver names, AAOI has the clearest single-story torque: one revenue ramp, one customer concentration question, one earnings print that settles it. The desk's read is that the 40–60% drawdown transferred most of the froth out of the position while the claimed monthly run-rate trajectory — roughly $471M/month at target — remained untested rather than disproven. An AMD CW long-term-agreement relationship gives the story a second leg beyond the hyperscaler transceiver ramp.

sections.heading: Risks
sections.body:
The run-rate story is still narrative until an earnings print validates it — 800% growth claims fail more often than they land. The analyst is heavily concentrated and openly suffering drawdowns here, so reiterations carry maximum book-talking risk; the desk discounts frequency and weights only new datapoints. And AAOI is the highest-beta expression of a complex that just demonstrated it can lose half its value on two blog posts.

AAOI is now an explicit sell-side battleground: B. Riley's sell case argues network flattening cuts transceiver TAM 40-50%, while Rosenblatt counters that XPU count grows faster than per-XPU optics shrink, so total optical demand still rises. Expect headline whipsaw into the print.

sections.heading: Bottom line
sections.body: Accumulate — scale in on the washout rather than paying up after the earnings confirmation, but keep half the intended size in reserve for the print. The new sell-report noise is not a thesis change; it raises the stakes on the earnings event that was always the decisive test of the $471m/month run-rate claim.

memos.title: A dated catalyst inside a boring giant
memos.dek: Jabil's 1.6T optical module qualification — Aug 2026 to Mar 2027, then mass production with Sivers lasers — gives the conservative photonics expression an actual clock.

sections.heading: Snapshot
sections.body: $JBL is a contract electronics giant whose relevance to this map is one program: it builds 1.6T LRO optical modules for AI datacenters, feeding the same interconnect demand as the pure plays in [[photonics]] — with a diversified multi-billion-revenue base underneath instead of a single-product balance sheet.

sections.heading: Thesis
sections.body:
This week produced the book's most concrete new datapoint, and it attaches here. Per a JPM fireside chat the analyst relayed, JBL management described a "relatively dramatic moat" in its 1.6T LRO, with qualification completing over the following months — an **Aug 2026 to Mar 2027** window — and mass production starting afterward, using $SIVE lasers in the pluggables. That converts JBL from a vague conservative-expression idea into a thesis with a dated, checkable catalyst.

It also makes JBL the keystone of the analyst's photonics stack: the same qualification event de-risks his SIVE position, which is worth remembering when weighing his enthusiasm.

sections.heading: Why own it
sections.body: It is the low-risk door into the highest-conviction corner of the book. If 1.6T optics ramp, JBL participates with manufacturing scale no pure play can match; if they slip, JBL remains a profitable EMS business and the position costs patience, not capital. The catalyst is verifiable from public sources — qualification completion and optics revenue commentary on the next two earnings calls — so the thesis does not depend on trusting anyone's enthusiasm.

sections.heading: Risks
sections.body: Dilution of impact is the structural one: optics can succeed and still be too small to move a ~$28B-revenue company's needle — the bull case needs optics broken out or visibly bending the growth rate. Program risk is the sharp one: qualification slipping past Q1 2027 or losing transceiver share to rival EMS suppliers guts the thesis. And note the sourcing: the moat language is management's own, relayed secondhand through the analyst — friendlier telephone than a filed disclosure.

sections.heading: Bottom line
sections.body: Accumulate — the starter position stands, with the qualification window as the concrete thing to verify across the next two earnings calls. Add if optics contribution starts getting broken out or the qual completes on schedule. Slip past Q1 2027 and the desk folds this back to watch.

memos.title: The substrate chokepoint that already re-rated
memos.dek: AXT owns roughly 40% of the indium-phosphide substrate supply the entire laser chain grows on — but the venture-scale move already happened, and the desk is on watch, not chase.

sections.heading: Snapshot
sections.body: $AXTI (AXT Inc) manufactures compound-semiconductor substrates — the indium-phosphide (InP) and gallium-arsenide wafers that photonics lasers are physically grown on. In the analyst's framing it controls roughly **40% of the InP substrate supply chain**, sitting one layer upstream of every laser maker in the [[photonics]] story, from [[sive]] to [[lite]].

sections.heading: Thesis
sections.body:
Every CW laser, EML and epiwafer in the optical ramp starts life on an InP substrate, and supply is concentrated in a handful of names. The confirmed anchor: a 3-year wafer supply deal with Coherent ([[cohr]]), including a **$22,288,500 prepayment** to AXT-Tongmei for committed capacity (8-K, 25 Jun). Around it, a Nomura report he relayed cites 2-inch InP substrate price hikes of 42-76%, 3-inch hikes of ~78%, and 50-75% on EML epiwafers — the [[chokepoint]] pattern this desk tracks across the map.

The desk's caution is about what's already in the price, not the mechanism: part of the ASP spike is a China export-control artifact (licenses began flowing in June 2026, and AXT is doubling capacity), so the hikes can normalize rather than compound.

sections.heading: Why own it
sections.body: It is the purest upstream expression of the optical build-out: if transceiver and CPO volumes ramp the way the [[photonics]] names require, substrate demand rises with all of them regardless of which laser vendor wins. The Coherent prepayment is exactly what a real chokepoint looks like — customers paying eight figures up front for committed capacity.

sections.heading: Risks
sections.body: The re-rating already happened — his own post describes the stock going 'from $1 to $60' (it peaked well above that), so today's buyer is not getting the trade his original thesis sized. The ASP-spike leg can unwind as export licenses normalize supply. His standing dilution warning applies most sharply here, and this week's three mentions were all list-posts plus a bare 'still holding' — mention-count momentum, not new evidence.

sections.heading: Bottom line
sections.body: Watch — the chokepoint is real but the venture-scale move is behind it. Re-engage on a real reset (a 30-40% retrace toward the run's base) with substrate ASPs still rising; the Nomura hike schedule is the dashboard. Chasing list-post momentum after a hundred-bagger leg is how the [[conviction-tier]] system gets people hurt.

memos.title: The memory trade the market refuses to believe
memos.dek: SanDisk's Meta supply deal and a verified NAND price upcycle landed the same week memory stocks sold off — the desk opens coverage on the gap.

sections.heading: Snapshot
sections.body:
$SNDK is the NAND flash pure play spun out of Western Digital — one of a handful of companies worldwide that manufacture the flash memory AI datacenters are suddenly consuming at scale. It enters desk coverage this week because the [[memory]] repricing stopped being a narrative and became contracts: a long-term supply agreement with $META confirmed, sector-wide price hikes verified by third-party researchers, and a completed wafer-level burn-in benchmark at $AEHR with a "global leader in NAND flash" that [[aleabitoreddit]] reads as SanDisk.

Unusually for this book, the analyst has never written a dedicated SanDisk thesis — the name surfaces only inside his memory-macro posts. Coverage here rests on the mosaic, and the memo says so plainly.

sections.heading: Thesis
sections.body:
The claim is that AI inference demand has structurally repriced memory, and NAND is the least-believed leg of it. The analyst's recap cites a **5x general NAND flash price move this year** triggered by RAG and inference workloads, with Samsung and SK Hynix making emergency fab investments to chase it; TrendForce sees server DRAM contracts up 13–18% next quarter with Samsung asking 20%, and SLC NAND forecast up 120–170% into H2. Demand visibility is the new part: $MU has signed 16+ long-term agreements and $META contracted with SanDisk directly — hyperscalers are locking supply years out, which is what turns a price spike into a cycle.

His sharpest observation is the market's reaction: hikes confirmed, LTAs signed — and memory names sold off anyway. The desk agrees that gap is the trade, with one caveat he doesn't make: LTAs cut both ways, capping the seller's price capture as well as guaranteeing volume. Micron kept price ceilings in its agreements; SanDisk's terms are undisclosed.

sections.heading: Why own it
sections.body: It is the purest listed NAND exposure to a repricing that now has contract-backed demand visibility rather than spot-market hope. The $AEHR angle adds free optionality: a completed burn-in benchmark and a potential development agreement around HBF — high-bandwidth flash entering AI inference stacks — would put SanDisk inside the AI bill of materials, not just adjacent to it. And unlike the DRAM majors, a NAND pure play doesn't dilute the cycle across HBM, logic, and foundry exposure.

sections.heading: Risks
sections.body: Three real ones. First, the mosaic problem: no dedicated thesis from the analyst means no falsifiable single claim to track — this coverage leans on third-party price data more than his work. Second, LTA terms: if SanDisk's agreements cap pricing well below spot, the upcycle accrues to the spot-heavy players instead. Third, the supply response: Samsung and SK Hynix emergency NAND capex is exactly how memory cycles die — if that capacity lands into 2027 demand that has normalized, the cycle inverts fast. The analyst himself flagged the stock approaching $2,000 with shorts wiped out — the run has already happened once, and post-run volatility in this name is violent.

sections.heading: Bottom line
sections.body: Accumulate — but entry discipline is most of the position. Starter tranche into the current memory weakness only, sized small; no chasing green days. Add if the $AEHR HBF development agreement is confirmed as SanDisk or if LTA pricing terms turn out to reset with spot. What sends this back to watch: the 2027 NAND capex wave arriving into softening demand, or the LTA terms revealing capped upside. The desk's fuller reasoning sits in the [[memory]] theme note and the verdict on the watchlist.

memos.title: A venture bet wearing a ticker
memos.dek: Agility Robotics' SPAC is the analyst's most concentrated robotics conviction — and the desk's clearest case of a real asset inside a dangerous structure.

sections.heading: Snapshot
sections.body:
$CCXI is the SPAC that becomes $AGLT around September — Agility Robotics, the US humanoid maker whose Digit robots are already deployed inside $AMZN and MELI warehouses, with Amazon, $NVDA, SoftBank and Foxconn on the cap table. The analyst ranks it the #1 US humanoid player by commercialization today (ahead of $TSLA Optimus and Figure) and has said outright he's 'trying to own as much % of Agility as possible' — his highest robotics concentration.

The desk covers it because the [[robotics]] theme now has institutional scaffolding — an SVRC 'State of Robotics' report lists Agility second among US national champions, and an IBK value-chain report supplies the tracking framework — but rates it below his conviction, deliberately.

sections.heading: Thesis
sections.body:
The macro leg is geopolitical: China ships ~90% of the world's humanoid robots, and a US champion is politically and commercially valuable the moment Washington notices. The micro leg is valuation relativity: Agility came public-market-accessible at roughly **$3–4B pre-money** while Figure — a less commercialized, general-purpose competitor — was last valued at **$39B** privately. Add a RoboFab targeting 10K+ units/year with ~75% US-sourced components (against Tesla's China-dependent bill of materials), and a planned deployment path through the warehouse customers it already serves.

Institutions are arriving on schedule: BlueCrest disclosed a 5.6% stake within days of the announcement. The forward indicator the desk tracks, per the IBK framework, is actuator capacity — roughly 310K actuators per 10K robots — which reads on the whole theme's ramp, not just Agility's.

sections.heading: Why own it
sections.body: It is the only public, pure-play access to a US humanoid program at a valuation still anchored near its private round — the exact retail-access structure the analyst praises Unitree for. If the S-curve he sketches (his 2030 guess: 90–140K units industry-wide versus institutions modeling ~30K) is even directionally right, the commercialized player inside Amazon's deployment machine is positioned to take the volume before the general-purpose labs ship anything.

sections.heading: Risks
sections.body: Start with the messenger: this is maximum talking-his-book territory — he owns it heavily, says so, and posts about it constantly, so the mention count badly overstates independent signal. Then the structure: it is still a SPAC, and his own critique of $BOT (trading at 3.5x its published NAV) applies uncomfortably well to any de-SPAC riding theme euphoria — dilution at the ticker change, a float that hasn't settled, and a market he himself describes as an 'ADHD goldfish' rotating through hot themes. Finally the industry: China's price war in humanoids can crush unit economics before US production scales.

sections.heading: Bottom line
sections.body: Watch — not because the asset is fake, but because the structure and the euphoria phase price it as if execution were done. If it must be owned, size it as venture risk: small, no adds on spikes, and let the post-SPAC float settle first. Upgrades on a funded, dated Amazon deployment contract or a US robotics subsidy program; downgrades on dilution at the $AGLT conversion or the SPAC closing below the cited valuation. The desk's fuller theme view lives in [[robotics]].

memos.title: The purest neocloud, graded by its customers
memos.dek: Nebius keeps winning exactly the contracts its thesis predicted — the desk's job now is grading the counterparties signing them.

sections.heading: Snapshot
sections.body: $NBIS is the analyst's highest-conviction [[neoclouds]] name and one of his oldest calls in this corpus — 'the purest Neocloud with execution certainty,' flagged as a potential next hyperscaler back when that was contrarian. The thesis has been repeatedly market-validated since: hyperscaler compute scarcity keeps forcing the giants to rent, and Nebius keeps signing them.

sections.heading: Thesis
sections.body:
The engine is structural compute scarcity at the top of the stack. The clarifying episode: $GOOGL restricted $META's cloud capacity in March 2026 because Google itself was compute-constrained — its cloud backlog nearly doubled in a quarter — which forced Meta into **$48B+ take-or-pay contracts** with neoclouds including Nebius. When hyperscalers can't rely on each other, the independent GPU cloud is the release valve.

The latest proof is diversification beyond hyperscalers: a **$1B+ agreement with Reflection AI** for GB300 access through 2029. And when the market read Meta's excess-compute-sales plan as neocloud-negative, Wells Fargo and Morgan Stanley read it the analyst's way — as validation of underlying AI demand, with take-or-pay contracts insulating the existing base.

sections.heading: Why own it
sections.body: It is the most direct listed expression of the gap between AI compute demand and hyperscaler capacity — with revenue locked in take-or-pay form rather than spot pricing. The customer mix is broadening on schedule: hyperscalers, then AI labs, then enterprises, each signing multi-year commitments. Every capex raise at $META or $MSFT tightens the scarcity that fills Nebius's order book.

sections.heading: Risks
sections.body: Counterparty quality is now the live one, and the analyst flags it himself: Reflection AI signed multi-billion commitments across several providers and 'might not have the funds to actually execute on these LTAs' — the OpenAI-commitment pattern in miniature. Second, Meta selling excess compute makes a hyperscaler a *competitor* for marginal neocloud demand, which is a structural threat to pricing power even if existing contracts are safe. Third, the model is financing-heavy and contract-concentrated; a debt raise on worse terms changes the risk math faster than the narrative will admit.

sections.heading: Bottom line
sections.body: Accumulate — but grade every new contract by who signed it. Fundable counterparties (hyperscalers, funded enterprises) repricing committed capacity are add signals; model-lab paper commitments are headline risk wearing a press release. What sends this to watch: hyperscaler capex cuts, Meta's resold compute visibly undercutting neocloud pricing, or a major counterparty failing to fund its commitments.

memos.title: The memory leader the desk won't chase
memos.dek: Micron has the cycle, the contracts and the scale — which is exactly why the marginal upside lives elsewhere in the memory book.

sections.heading: Snapshot
sections.body: $MU is the US memory flagship — DRAM, HBM and NAND — and the name the market reaches for first when the [[memory]] theme works. The analyst treats every MU dip as a buying opportunity and cites his gross-margin model being 'validated ahead of time' as DRAM/NAND hikes landed. The desk added this verdict at operator request, and rates it below his enthusiasm on purpose.

sections.heading: Thesis
sections.body:
The demand case is contract-hardened: **16+ long-term agreements** signed, plus a new LTA with $QCOM announced this week — and the stock fell 5%+ on that news, which he reads as the tail end of margin-cascade deleveraging rather than anything fundamental. Pricing momentum is verified from multiple directions: UBS models DDR contract prices **+32% in Q3 and +18% in Q4** (above prior estimates) with NAND +30%; Samsung is seeking 20% DRAM hikes; TrendForce sees server DRAM up 13–18%. On the supply side MU is leading a ~¥1.5 trillion investment in Hiroshima — capex into a cycle it has pre-sold.

His rotation frame says memory and photonics alternate as the market's favorite bottleneck, with memory 'bottlenecked into 2029.' Nothing this week contradicts that.

sections.heading: Why own it
sections.body: It is the broadest, lowest-idiosyncratic-risk expression of the memory upcycle: US-listed, mega-cap, diversified across DRAM, HBM and NAND, with demand visibility that smaller players can't match. If the upcycle is real and you want one memory name you never have to babysit, this is the candidate.

sections.heading: Risks
sections.body: The desk's reservation is about upside capture, not safety. Micron retained **price ceilings and floors** in its strategic customer agreements — while SK Hynix reportedly scrapped its caps — so the flagship participates less in the spot-price spike than the headline hikes suggest. Scale cuts the same way: on the sharpest sub-cycles the analyst himself points at Winbond, Macronix and SkyHigh as the cleaner beneficiaries because MU's ~$1T market position dwarfs the affected TAM. And his MU conviction is his weakest form of signal — dip-buy remarks and list-post appearances, never a dedicated deep dive with numbers.

sections.heading: Bottom line
sections.body: Watch. The desk's memory torque is already expressed through $SNDK, where the NAND repricing bites hardest and the market's disbelief is deepest; MU is the consensus way in, and consensus is priced. Upgrades on evidence its LTA terms participate in spot upside, or a dedicated MU thesis from the analyst. If DRAM contract momentum breaks, the whole memory book downgrades — SNDK first, this name second.

memos.title: Capacity into an upgraded cycle — and a boundary drawn
memos.dek: Innolight revised 800G demand upward the same week AAOI broke ground in Texas; the desk also now knows exactly what this bet is not.

sections.heading: Snapshot
sections.body: Two things changed since the last memo, one bullish and one clarifying. Bullish: Innolight's investor relations revised **800G transceiver demand sharply upward** ('demand has not contracted; instead, 800G demand has increased significantly'), with new customer types — neoclouds, AI model companies — broadening the base beyond hyperscalers. Clarifying: the analyst's own channel work concedes $AAOI has **no CPO design wins**, with the CEO calling commercial timing 'TBD.' This is a pluggables business, full stop.

sections.heading: Thesis
sections.body: The bet is torque to the 800G/1.6T pluggable cycle, with capacity arriving into demand that was just revised up. AAOI formally commenced its ~400,000 sq ft Texas manufacturing buildout, supported by state and local subsidies, on top of the prior $20.85M package. His $471m/month H2-2027 revenue framing remains the bull anchor; the Innolight revision is the first outside datapoint since the bear report that leans his way. The bull/bear fight (B. Riley's network-flattening sell case versus Rosenblatt's XPU-count counter) is unresolved and makes the earnings print the decisive event.

sections.heading: Why own it
sections.body: Among the [[photonics]] names, AAOI is the highest-torque way to own the pluggable demand revision: smaller than $LITE/$COHR, capacity growing into the cycle, and now with a demand datapoint (800G revised up, longer tail) that directly feeds its product mix. The subsidized buildout lowers the capital cost of being wrong on timing.

sections.heading: Risks
sections.body: The no-CPO clarification is a genuine boundary: if the industry transition to co-packaged optics comes faster than the 800G tail sustains, AAOI is on the wrong side of the architecture with no design-win hedge — the exact scenario where $SIVE/$LITE/$COHR win and pluggable pure-plays lose. It is also now a named battleground stock; expect headline whipsaw into the print, in both directions. Execution risk on a 400k sq ft ramp is real for a company this size.

sections.heading: Bottom line
sections.body: Accumulate, unchanged: hold the scale-in from the washout, keep the reserved half for the earnings print. Treat CPO headlines — good and bad — as noise for AAOI specifically; the variables that matter are 800G/1.6T pluggable volume and the Texas ramp timeline. Back to watch on an earnings miss against the monthly run-rate narrative, or on evidence CPO is cannibalizing pluggables faster than the revised demand supports.

memos.title: The trio is now official
memos.dek: Morgan Stanley put Sivers beside Lumentum and Coherent as the CPO laser leaders — the anonymity discount the accumulation plan harvests has started expiring.

sections.heading: Snapshot
sections.body: The week the sell side caught up: Morgan Stanley's CPO note names $SIVE alongside $LITE and $COHR as the laser-supply leaders — a ~$1.5B Swedish-listed company placed next to two $60B+ incumbents, which is the institutional validation of the three-leader framing the analyst built from his own OSINT months ago. The field simultaneously narrowed: MACOM has fallen off the CPO laser map and $AAOI concedes no CPO design wins, so the trio is not just named, it's shrinking toward itself.

sections.heading: Thesis
sections.body:
Unchanged in structure, stronger in evidence. The multi-track qualification web — $GFS reference laser, Ayar Labs in the $NVDA NVLink CPO scale-up, $POET scale-out, $JBL 1.6T pluggables, O-Net for ELS mass production — means four or five partially independent paths to volume, any one of which validates the ramp, on his cited ~60% gross margins with Win Semi as manufacturing partner. Demand context hardened too: Innolight's rising silicon-photonics mix shifts industry demand from EML toward exactly the CW lasers Sivers makes, and the sold-out-into-2029 environment at LITE/COHR is now triple-sourced.

His market-structure point — algos miss a name on 'some random Swedish exchange' and it catches up in bursts — is the accumulation window itself, and the MS naming is the beginning of its end.

sections.heading: Why own it
sections.body: Still the same asymmetric structure: a genuine bottleneck owned by a small company, multiple independent qualification tracks, and a non-fundamental catalyst (the NASDAQ listing) that re-rates liquidity without needing the technology thesis to resolve first. What's new is that institutional discovery has visibly begun — which raises the odds the re-rate happens before the volume numbers force it.

sections.heading: Risks
sections.body: Every flag from the first memo stays live, and they are serious: the auditor's going-concern language around the SEK 700M raise, the Ningi short report, dilution risk if cash strain is real, and the integrated-laser (OpenLight-style) architecture kill-shot that removes discrete CW suppliers from the diagram entirely. The messenger risk is maximal — he holds a million-plus shares and says so; at 80+ mentions, this corpus is as much advocacy as analysis. Sell-side validation cuts both ways: institutional attention also means institutional scrutiny of the balance sheet.

sections.heading: Bottom line
sections.body: Accumulate on the staggered plan, unchanged — but the clock is now visible. The ignored-listing discount closes as coverage arrives, so the plan's remaining tranches want red days taken when offered rather than waited for, and the position wants to be built before the NASDAQ listing event. Kill-switches unchanged: insider selling resuming, the listing slipping without explanation, the [[jbl]]/GlobalFoundries tracks failing to convert, or SIVE losing its slot in the trio the sell side just defined.
